coredata.dc:title: Pennisteum glaucum-derived carbon dots, synthesized through green approach to degrade Rose Bengal and Methylene Blue dyes photocatalytically
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Tripti,
Kumar, Sandeep
Kumar, Parmod
coredata.dc:description: In the present study, carbon dots (C-dots) were synthesized using a simple and uncomplicated pyrolysis method at 250 °C with varying duration of 1 h, 2 h, and 3 h, utilizing Pennisetum glaucum as the biomass precursor to break down methylene blue (MB) and Rose Bengal (RB) dyes under UV light exposure. Various analytical techniques, including X-ray diffraction (XRD), UV–visible spectroscopy, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and vibrating-sample magnetometer (VSM), were utilized to investigate the chemical, optical, electronic, surface morphological, and magnetic characteristics of the C-dots produced. These C-dots are crystalline, nearly spherical, and have particle sizes ranging from 4.73 nm to 12.44 nm. FTIR analysis identified the functional groups present on the surface of the C-dots. Additionally, the band gap energy of the C-dots reduced from 2.82 eV to 2.40 eV with an increase in pyrolysis time. They are effective for cleaning dye-polluted wastewater and demonstrate high photocatalytic performance. When exposed to UV light, the synthesized particles achieved impressive photocatalytic degradation of 94.80 % for MB and 91.80 % for RB dyes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photocatalysis
Degradation efficiency
Wastewater

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Tripti acknowledges Department of Physics, J.C. Bose university of Science and Technology, YMCA, Faridabad, Haryana, India, for providing the platform for this study. P Kumar and S Kumar would like to acknowledge UGC, New Delhi for support under UGC-STARTUP (F.30-589/2021(BSR)) and Department of Science and Technology, New Delhi for DST-PURSE grant (SR/PURSE/2022/126).

Tripti acknowledges Department of Physics, J.C. Bose university of Science and Technology, YMCA, Faridabad, Haryana, India, for providing the platform for this study. P Kumar would like to acknowledge UGC , New Delhi for support under UGC-STARTUP ( F.30-589/2021(BSR) ) and Department of Science and Technology , New Delhi for DST-PURSE grant ( SR/PURSE/2022/126 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD analysis of C-dots derived from Pennisetum glaucum (bajra).
HRTEM images of as-synthesized C-dots (a) B 1 h, (b) B 2 h, and (c) B 3 h and their respective histogram illustrating particle size distribution (a) B 1 h, (b) B 2 h, and (c) B 3 h.
UV–vis absorbance spectra (a) B 1 h, (b) B 2 h, (c) B 3 h, and Tauc plot (d) B 1 h, (e) B 2 h, (f) B 3 h of as-synthesized C-dots.
Magnetization curve observed at room temperature.
Degradation % of (a) MB and (b) RB dyes with time for different C-dots (B 1 h, B 2 h and B 3 h).
Decrement in the prominent peak of (a) MB and (b) RB dye with time.
Plot of experimental data and linear fit of -ln(A/A˳) against time.
Bar chart showing the electricity cost in Indian rupees for MB and RB dye degradation.
Basic mechanism of photocatalytic decolorization of RB and MB dye using as-prepared C-dots.

(a) Relative degradation of MB dye, (b) comparison of % MB dye degradation in the presence of various scavengers: EDTA for h+, i-PA for OH, and p-BQ for O2 −, (c) relative degradation of MB dye, (d) comparison of % MB dye degradation in the presence of various scavengers.
Synthesis route of C-dots using Pennisetum glaucum.
Visual representation of photocatalytic degradation of dyes.
The range of particle sizes and the mean diameter of the synthesized C-dots.
Parameters regarding maximum photocatalytic degradation of MB and RB dye using C-dots (B 3 h) as photocatalyst.

head.ce:title: Pennisteum glaucum-derived carbon dots, synthesized through green approach to degrade Rose Bengal and Methylene Blue dyes photocatalytically

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: J. C. Bose University of Science & Technology, YMCA, Faridabad 126006, Haryana, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: J. C. Bose University of Science & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: J. C. Bose University of Science & Technology, YMCA, Faridabad-126006, Haryana, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Gurugram University, Gurugram, 122003, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Gurugram University Gurugram, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: P. Kumar, J. C. Bose University of Science & Technology, YMCA, Faridabad 126006, Haryana, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: J. C. Bose University of Science & Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, carbon dots (C-dots) were synthesized using a simple and uncomplicated pyrolysis method at 250 °C with varying duration of 1 h, 2 h, and 3 h, utilizing Pennisetum glaucum as the biomass precursor to break down methylene blue (MB) and Rose Bengal (RB) dyes under UV light exposure. Various analytical techniques, including X-ray diffraction (XRD), UV–visible spectroscopy, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and vibrating-sample magnetometer (VSM), were utilized to investigate the chemical, optical, electronic, surface morphological, and magnetic characteristics of the C-dots produced. These C-dots are crystalline, nearly spherical, and have particle sizes ranging from 4.73 nm to 12.44 nm. FTIR analysis identified the functional groups present on the surface of the C-dots. Additionally, the band gap energy of the C-dots reduced from 2.82 eV to 2.40 eV with an increase in pyrolysis time. They are effective for cleaning dye-polluted wastewater and demonstrate high photocatalytic performance. When exposed to UV light, the synthesized particles achieved impressive photocatalytic degradation of 94.80 % for MB and 91.80 % for RB dyes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The Industrial Revolution has significantly advanced the modernization of production processes and consumption patterns in the current generation. Industries represent the primary source of emerging contaminants entering environmental components [1]. The challenge of environmental pollution, particularly water pollution stands as a critical issue. Organic dyes and their effluents represent a significant contributor to this issue, which are generally discharged from conventional wastewater treatment plants, mining to textiles, paper printing to food processing, cosmetics to pharmaceuticals, and leather industries [2–5]. Dye residues and their associated by-products are known to be carcinogenic and harmful to microorganisms, humans, and marine life [6]. Approximately 6 to 8 million organisms lose their lives because of water-related diseases and disasters, annually [7]. Presently, the textile industry alone utilizes nearly 3.6 × 107 kg of dye per annum and production of dyes hits 100,000 varieties annually [8]. In the dyeing process, a significant portion, up to 20 % of the global dye production, is lost and released as effluents in textile wastewater [9]. Therefore, using an effective and economical approach, proper wastewater treatment is essential for maintaining environmental purity. For decontamination of dye-polluted wastewater, many techniques have been employed like biodegradation [10,11], membrane process [12,13], adsorption [14,15], ion-exchange coagulation/flocculation [16,17], desalination [18,19], advanced oxidation approaches [20], and reverse osmosis [21,22], to reduce their adverse effects. But unluckily, these methods have several limitations such as excessive generation of unsafe byproducts, costly, delayed reaction, rigid, high repair cost, and lesser degradation efficiency [1,23–25]. Photocatalysis technology has proven to be efficient for air decontamination, Sewage management, and purification. In recent years, there has been significant interest in the use of photocatalytic processes to degrade organic compounds as a means of purifying wastewater generated by both industrial and household activities [26]. Photocatalysis has the potential to reduce environmental issues as it can decolorize a wide spectrum of harmful compounds with exposure of sunlight [27].

Over the past few years, research has demonstrated that nanostructured carbon materials possessing strong photocatalytic decomposition and adsorption properties effectively remove biological contaminants from the atmosphere and river. Albiss et al. described the photocatalytic degradation of MB dye utilizing zinc oxide nanorods grown on activated carbon fibers synthesized through a hydrothermal method, achieving a maximum degradation of 99 % within 2 h [28]. Alharthi and colleagues reported the synthesis of graphitic carbon nitride-doped zinc oxide nanocomposites. These nanocomposites were employed as photocatalysts for the mineralization of the light-sensitive dyes Methylene Blue (MB) and Rose Bengal (RB) under UV light irradiation. The maximum degradation achieved was 90 % for both dyes within 6 h and 4 h, respectively [29]. Ameena et al. synthesized polyaniline/graphene nanocomposites through the in situ polymerization of aniline monomer with graphene for the photocatalytic degradation of RB dye under a 300 W Xe lamp. The maximum degradation reached was 56 % within 3 h [30]. Hui et al. reported the use of nitrogen- and bismuth-doped carbon quantum dots derived from rice husk for the photocatalytic degradation of MB dye and the removal of heavy metals. The degradation results were approximately 72 % and 68 % using nitrogen- and bismuth-doped CQDs, respectively, under a 300 W Xe lamp [31]. Among these carbon materials, C-dots emerge as highly beneficial elements in the photocatalytic purification of wastewater [32]. C-dots are derived as small carbon nanomaterials in 2004 by Xu et al. [33], semi-spherical nanoparticles of dimensions less than 10 nm [34]. They are having minimal toxicity, enhanced biocompatibility, excellent aqueous solubility, admirable photostability, and surface passivation is greatly enhanced through functionalization with organic or bio-molecules [35–37]. Because of their strong and programmable fluorescence emission characteristics, C-dots boast a diverse range of applications, including bio-sensing, bioimaging, radionuclide detection, and LED technology [38,39].

Methylene blue (MB) is a vibrant dye primarily used in textiles. In the year 1800, Heinrich Caro initiated its synthesis [40]. MB is a versatile dye broadly applied in diverse industries, including textile, rubber, plastics, leather, pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, and the food industry. Its primary use lies in providing coloration and staining functionalities in these various sectors [41]. MB presents various health concerns for individuals. Certain nations regard MB as hazardous because it can lead to skin irritation and provoke an unwanted inflammatory reaction. MB has dose-related toxic effects, such as haemolysis, methemoglobinemia, nausea, vomiting, chest discomfort, breathing difficulties, high blood pressure, skin irritation, and the initiation of an adverse inflammatory reaction [42]. Rose Bengal (RB) dyes find widespread usage in laboratories and industries such as leather, food, textile, and pharmaceuticals as coloring agents. However, these dyes exhibit nonbiodegradable properties and are highly toxic, teratogenic, carcinogenic, and mutagenic [43]. RB dye can cause skin irritation, allergic reactions, and eye irritation upon contact. Prolonged exposure or ingestion may lead to potential toxicity, including adverse effects on human health and the environment. Improper disposal can also contribute to environmental contamination, impacting aquatic life and ecosystems [44,45]. Several researchers have reported the degradation of RB and MB dye using photocatalysts. Bhattacharjee et al. reported 94.5 % photocatalytic degradation of MB dye in 120 min using C-dots derived from sawdust under the irradiation of 23 W white LED bulb [46]. Cailotto and colleagues produced C-dots doped with nitrogen derived from beer waste and demonstrated complete breakdown of methylene blue within 24 h using these carbon dots by UV light illumination [47].

In the current investigation, we synthesized C-dots from Pennisetum glaucum (bajra) via a simple, productive, and cost-effective pyrolysis method for different time intervals 1 h, 2 h, and 3 h at 250 °C. As-synthesized C-dots were employed for degradation of wastewater contaminated with MB and RB. The C-dots exhibited excellent performance of degradation for both dyes having a removal % of 94.80 for MB dye and 91.80 % for RB dye within UV light exposure for 90 min and 120 min, sequentially. The current state of research suggests that there are only a few publications regarding, how the magnetic properties change over time while pyrolyzing biomass-derived C-dots, and their degradation efficacy in MB and RB dyes. The main goal of this research is to understand the reasons behind the time-related paramagnetic and photocatalytic properties of C-dots synthesized in an environmentally friendly manner through pyrolysis.

In this study, the precursor Pennisetum glaucum (bajra) was permitted to dry naturally under solar heat for about 24 h. Later, the precursor was thermally pyrolyzed in a muffle furnace for various intervals- 1 h, 2 h, and 3 h at 250 °C. The specimens obtained post-pyrolysis were separately grinded into an extremely fine powder. The derived powder was mixed separately with 250 mL of redistilled water in a canonical flask and then mixed at 1050 rpm for 12 h at room temperature. After stirring, the resulting solutions were left undisturbed for 24 h prior to centrifugation for 1 h at 4000 rpm to remove larger molecules. Subsequently, the solution undergoes filtration, and the resulting supernatant undergoes dehydration to attain the intended final product. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , this process resulted in the generation of C-dots. As-synthesized C-dots derived from bajra were denoted as B 1 h, B 2 h, and B 3 h, respectively, where “B” represents the raw material (bajra), and “1 h,” “2 h,” and “3 h” denote the respective pyrolysis time.

The structural characteristics of the fabricated C-dots were analyzed using a Rigaku Ultima IV X-ray instrument equipped with monochromatized CuKα radiation (wavelength: 1.548 Å). Scans were conducted at a speed of 0.02°/min within the 10–80-degree range. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was employed to investigate functional groups within the range of 500–4000 cm−1 using a PERKIN spectrometer (serial number: 118007). Cryo high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was employed to examine the morphology of the samples. To investigate the optical and photocatalytic properties, a UV–vis analysis was performed using a SHIMADZU UV 3600i plus UV–vis spectrophotometer. The magnetic properties were measured by Vibrating Sample Magnetometer (VSM) by microsense, Model ADE- EV9 with a sensitivity level of 10−5 emu at room temperature.

The breakdown of MB and RB dyes were achieved using as synthesized C-dots. To perform this, solution of definite concentration of MB and RB dyes were prepared in deionized water. On adding definite amount of C-dots to 50 mL of dye solution, it was followed by continuous stirring in dark for about 40 min to achieve adsorption desorption equilibrium. As a light source, a 300 W UV bulb at a height of 25 cm. Over time, 3 mL samples were taken at fixed intervals and the degradation was analyzed using a UV–visible spectrophotometer. The peak absorption intensity for RB dye occurred at 547 nm, while for MB dye, it was detected at 664 nm (Scheme 2 ).

The XRD analysis depicted in Fig. 1 confirms the crystalline nature of the synthesized C-dots. This pattern was compared with a standard graphitic carbon diffraction pattern to establish a correlation. The C-dots displayed prominent peaks at around 21.7° and 24.1°, corresponding to the (311) and (222) planes respectively [48]. Additional peaks at approximately 28.4°, 31.8°, and 40.6° corresponded to crystal planes (002), (211), and (100), indicating sp2 hybridized graphitic carbon. Peaks at around 56.5° and 66.4° corresponded to the (600) and (220) planes of sp3 hybridized carbon atoms in the diamond structure. Furthermore, peaks at approximately 45.4° and 75.2° were observed, corresponding to the (111) and (215) planes respectively [49]. It has been noted that as the pyrolysis duration increases, both the number and intensity of peaks in the XRD pattern decrease. This observation suggests that longer pyrolysis times produce C-dots with less crystallinity.

FTIR spectroscopy was performed to examine the synthesized C-dots' chemical composition and functional groups. As shown in Fig. 2 , the peak between 3315 and 3272 cm−1 is ascribed to the lengthening vibrations of -OH [45]; the peak between 2929 and 2886 cm−1 corresponds to the CH group [46]; the peaks at 2339 and 2231 cm−1 are associated with the CH overtone [37]; the peak at 1641 cm−1 is assigned to the CO group [47]; peaks at 1086 cm−1 and 1038 cm−1 are due to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching of C-O-C, respectively [48]. Peaks at 895 cm−1 and 722 cm−1 are indicative of CC and CH bonds. A peak in the range of 582 to 496 cm−1 is attributed to aromatic sp2 CH bending in C-dots [49].

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were used to obtain important details about the size, morphology, and shape of the synthesized samples. The particle size of the samples was calculated by using ImageJ software, taking an average of more than 50 particles. Fig. 2 displays the HR-TEM pictures of the synthesized C-dots along with their corresponding histograms. The C-dots appear spherical, with average diameters ranging from 4.73 to 12.44 nm as listed in Table 1 . Among these samples, the smallest average particle size was observed for sample B 3 h (4.73 nm), while the largest was found for B 1 h (12.44 nm) (Fig. 3).

UV–Vis absorbance spectra analysis was performed to investigate the electronic band framework of C-dots. The absorbance wavelength of C-dots was observed in the range of 281 nm to 288 nm, corresponding to n-π* transitions of the CO bond and π-π* transitions of the CC bond shown in Fig. 4 . Tauc plot method was employed to determine the band gap calculation of as-synthesized carbon dots and were found to be 2.82 eV, 2.50 eV, and 2.40 eV for samples B 1 h, B 2 h, and B 3 h, respectively. By adopting the following equation:
(1)
αhν
n
= k
hν − Eg
where symbols have their usual meaning and n = 1/2 as having an indirect band gap. Band gap was calculated by plotting ( αhν )
1/2 versus Eg as shown in Fig. 4.

The organized magnetic arrangement observed in metal-free carbon nanomaterials arises from the mutual interaction, or coupling, among unpaired electrons [50]. As a result, the magnetic ordering in C-dots occurs through the coupling of non-paired electrons, likely linked to the shell layer. Meanwhile, the characterization of the core area of C-dots reveals semiconductor properties with a bounded quantum state [51].

To evaluate the inherent magnetism of C-dots, their magnetic properties were analyzed using a VSM. The VSM analysis revealed a distinct hysteresis pattern of carbon's magnetization (M) with respect to the applied magnetic field, after subtracting the diamagnetic part, as depicted in Fig. 5 . At room temperature (300K), the saturation magnetization (Ms) values were 24.12 × 10−3 emu/g, 20.06 × 10−3 emu/g, and 18.72 × 10−3 emu/g, for samples labeled as B 1 h, B 2 h, and B 3 h, respectively, across the magnetic field range from −10 kOe to 10 kOe. The observations indicate that as the pyrolysis time increases, there is a decrease in the level of magnetism. The sample B 1 h exhibited the highest magnetization (Ms) value at 24.12 × 10−3 emu/g.

The photocatalytic degradation of MB and RB dyes was examined under UV light using C-dots, derived from Pennisetum glaucum by adopting a green synthesis method, as a catalyst. We conducted experiments to see how effectively the C-dots can remove the colour from the dyes, showing their photocatalytic ability. The degradation efficiency was determined using a double-beam UV–visible spectrophotometer.

The as-synthesized C-dots (B 1 h, B 2 h, B 3 h) were utilized for the photocatalytic degradation of MB and RB dyes. For the photocatalytic degradation, 5 mg of C-dots was used to degrade 50 mL of MB and RB dye solutions, each having a concentration of 5 ppm. A reduction in the absorption peak intensity at 664 nm for MB dye and 547 nm for RB dye over time indicates a decrease in dye concentration. It was observed that B 3 h had the highest degradation efficiency, degrading 94.80 % of MB dye within 90 min and 91.80 % of RB dye within 120 min, as depicted in Fig. 6 .

The enhanced photocatalytic efficiency of C-dots with extended pyrolysis times can be elucidated through HRTEM analysis. HRTEM studies demonstrated that as pyrolysis time increased, the particle size of the resulting C-dots decreased. This reduction in particle size significantly increases the overall surface area of C-dots from B 1 h to B 3 h. Also, prolonged pyrolysis leads to the increase of the sp2 network of C-dots, which probably facilitates the electrons' flow and could result in greater interactions with RB and MB (π-π), since both dyes bear aromatic systems in their structures [52]. Consequently, more dye molecules could be adsorbed onto the C-dots' surface due to the expanded surface area and the higher presence of surface-active functional groups [8]. This increased adsorption facilitated greater interaction between these enhanced surface-reactive sites and the dye molecules, resulting in more effective photocatalytic degradation of the dye. This phenomenon is likely the primary reason for the superior photocatalytic performance of B 3 h C-dots compared to B 1 h and B 2 h counterparts.

Photocatalytic decomposition of RB and MB dyes are represented in Fig. 7 using synthesized C-dots as catalysts. Degradation of dye is confirmed by observing a decrement in the intensity of prominent peak i.e. at 664 nm for MB dye and 547 nm for RB dye, within intervals of 90 min and 120 min, respectively. A small degradation/adsorption was observed for both dyes (i.e. 12.02 % for MB and 14.35 % for RB dye) in dark conditions (i.e. under the influence of catalyst alone) and insignificant degradation was observed in the presence of light only.

The photocatalytic degradation process followed first-order kinetics, as indicated by the linear correlation between ln(Ao/A) and time, as illustrated in the graphs (Fig. 8 ). This finding is strengthened by the correlation coefficient (R2) value. For first-order kinetics reaction, R2 value is within the range of 0.95–1. The equation below specifies a first-order reaction and its half-life [53].
(2)
ln
A ˳
A
= kt
(3)
t
1 / 2
=
0.693 k
(4)
Degradation % =
A ˳ − A
A ˳
× 100
where A˳ is initial concentration of dye, A is final concentration, and k is slope. k values for MB and RB dye were 0.0366 min−1 and 0.0231 min−1, respectively. The photocatalyst B 3 h showed remarkable degradation performance for both dyes, MB and RB, achieving 94.80 % (12.02 % in dark conditions +82.72 % under photocatalysis) for MB and 91.80 % (14.35 % in dark conditions +77.45 % under photocatalysis) for RB, respectively. The percentage eradication of MB and RB dyes using the synthesized B 3 h photocatalysts is calculated using Eq. (4). Furthermore, by utilizing the given formulas, the associated electricity cost for the purification of MB and RB dyes (as shown in Table 2 ) has been calculated [54].
(5)
E c
=
P ×
t
90 %
× 4.68
1000 × 60
(6)
t
90 %
=
t
1 / 2
0.301
where P is power of UV source (300 W), t90% is the time taken to degrade 90 % of the initial dye concentration. In our locality, the electricity rate is 4.68 INR per unit, with a maximum limit of 500 units. A higher degradation efficiency of the photocatalyst leads to reduced overall electricity expenses for the degradation process thus increasing the cost-effectiveness of the catalyst. Increasing pyrolysis interval for synthesizing C-dots decreases the electricity expense for MB and RB decolourisation. As the photocatalytic efficiency sequence of the attained C-dots is B 3 h > B 2 h > B 1 h, as shown in Fig. 6. Therefore, it becomes evident that as the duration of pyrolysis increases, the electricity cost decreases. Consequently, this makes the synthesized C-dots (B 3 h) as the most economically viable photocatalyst option (Fig. 9).

In this procedure, the degradation of harmful dyes is primarily induced by ultraviolet, visible, or infrared light sources with energy equal to or exceeding the bandgap energy of the photocatalyst, to initiate the breakdown of harmful dyes into less toxic or non-toxic molecules [55]. Initially, dye molecules are attached to the surface of photocatalyst, C-dots, to bring the dye molecules in close vicinity of photocatalyst to initiate further reaction. Leading to a generation of e−-h+ pairs in the valance band and conduction band, respectively, after UV light irradiation. These photoreactive species (e−-h+) participate in several redox reactions. h+ oxidizes dye molecules by removing e−, resulting in formation of reactive oxygen species including hydroxyl radicals (OH), and e− reduces O2 molecules to highly reactive superoxide anions (O2 −) [56,57] shown in Fig. 10 . These hydroxyl radicals prove highly efficient in breaking down dye into various non-toxic organic compounds, like CO2, H2O, and mineral ions. Basic mechanism route photocatalytic decolorization of organic dyes involves several chemical reactions, illustrated below from Eqs. (7) to (14):
Generation of electrons and holes:
(7)
C − dots + һν → C − dots
e −
CB
h +
VB
Oxidation:
(8)
h +
+
H 2
O → ˙ OH +
H +
Reduction:
(9)
e −
+
O 2
→ ˙
O 2
−
Superoxide protonation:
(10)
˙
O 2
−
+
H +
→ HOO ˙
Generation of hydrogen peroxide:
(11)
2HO ˙
2
→
H 2
O 2
+
O 2
Hydroxyl radical formation:
(12)
H 2
O 2
+
e −
CB
→ HO ∙ +
OH −
(13)
OH −
+
h +
VB
→ HO
Degradation of dye:
(14)
Dye + ˙ OH →
CO 2
+
H 2
O + by
product

ce:section.ce:section-title: The impact of scavenger presence on photocatalytic degradation of dye
ce:section.ce:para: When a photocatalyst is exposed to light, it generally produces three primary reactive species in the solution: electron holes, superoxide radicals (O2 −), and hydroxyl radicals (OH). These radicals play a crucial role in the photocatalytic degradation of dye pollutants. An experiment was conducted to specifically identify and study these reactive species, aiming to gain a comprehensive understanding of their roles in treating wastewater contaminated with dyes [58]. Separate experiments were performed to capture the h+, OH, and O2 − scavengers. Disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA) was used at a concentration of 10 mM as the h+ scavenger, while isopropyl alcohol (i-PA) at the same concentration acted as the OH scavenger. Para-benzoquinone (p-BQ) was employed as the O2 − scavenger [40].

It was observed that EDTA and i-PA effectively inhibited the photocatalytic degradation of both MB and RB dyes by capturing h+ and OH radicals, respectively. Conversely, p-BQ caused the least reduction in degradation. As shown in Fig. 11 , the percentage degradation decreased to approximately 51 %, 38 %, and 22 % for MB dye and around 43 %, 63 %, and 30 % for RB dye with the addition of EDTA, i-PA, and p-BQ, respectively. This indicates that O2 − and OH radicals play a primary role in the photocatalytic process, while h+ radicals are secondary participants in the degradation of MB dye. For RB dye, h+ and O2 − are the primary reactive species in photocatalysis, with OH radicals as secondary participants. Therefore, the order of reactive species involved in the photocatalytic degradation of MB dye is O2 − ˃ OH ˃ h+, and for RB dye, it is O2 − ˃ h+ ˃ OH.

Carbon dots (C-dots) obtained from Bajra (Pennisetum glaucum) underwent synthesis using an economical pyrolysis technique, with different durations for the pyrolysis process. XRD examination indicated a decrease in crystallinity as the pyrolysis duration extended. VSM investigation demonstrated paramagnetic characteristics at ambient temperature, with B 1 h displaying the strongest magnetic response (24.12 × 10−3 emu/g). Synthesized C-dots exhibited efficient photocatalytic degradation of MB and RB dyes, with various parameters such as rate constant, decolorization extent, half-life, and electricity costs were calculated. Longer pyrolysis times resulted in enhanced degradation rates for both dyes. The improved photocatalytic performance of B 3 h, which achieved maximum degradation of MB (94.80 %) and RB (91.80 %), was attributed to increased surface area and surface-active functional groups. Overall, the study concludes that C-dots exhibit increased photocatalytic activity with longer pyrolysis durations under UV light, showing promise for wastewater remediation contaminated with MB and RB dyes.

Tripti: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Sandeep Kumar: Supervision, Investigation, Formal analysis. Parmod Kumar: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there is no conflict of interest on work presented in the manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Tripti acknowledges Department of Physics, J.C. Bose university of Science and Technology, YMCA, Faridabad, Haryana, India, for providing the platform for this study. P Kumar and S Kumar would like to acknowledge UGC, New Delhi for support under UGC-STARTUP (F.30-589/2021(BSR)) and Department of Science and Technology, New Delhi for DST-PURSE grant (SR/PURSE/2022/126).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots as potential candidate for photocatalytic treatment of dye wastewater
